title: The US is driving away international students at a long-term economic cost
gist: PIIE projects that the Trump administration's policies excluding international students, which have already cut F-1 visa issuance by a third, would shrink US real GDP by $240-481 billion annually over a decade by starving the high-skill STEM workforce that drives productivity growth.

insights.claim: Since early 2025 the Trump administration has threatened to block federal funding for universities that do not limit international enrollment and banned student visas for nationals of 38 countries.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Watch university endowments, tuition-dependent institutions, and college-town real estate for revenue contraction as enrollment policy tightens.

insights.claim: Through September 2025 the US issued about a third fewer F-1 student visas than typical recent years, the steepest decline outside the 2020 pandemic.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: A measurable one-third drop is the baseline input for all downstream GDP-loss estimates; deeper cuts would amplify the economic damage.
insights.locator: This policy program has already slashed

insights.claim: The administration ended the duration-of-status rule, reweighted the H-1B lottery toward experienced workers (lowering odds for new graduates), and instructed officers to deny student adjustment to permanent residence except in extraordinary cases.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Employers reliant on retaining US-trained foreign graduates face a tighter pipeline; firms with high H-1B dependence (tech, semis) most exposed.
insights.locator: Second, US officials made it harder

insights.claim: In a PIIE survey of 1,039 current international students and postdocs, about half said they probably or definitely would never have come had they known about the new restrictions in advance.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Signals that deterrence is self-reinforcing; future inflows could fall well below the one-third decline already observed.
insights.locator: And students are getting the message

themes.theme: Foreign-born talent as backbone of US STEM

insights.claim: Foreign-born workers account for 30 percent of all US STEM workers and 49 percent of those whose highest degree is a PhD, with US-trained foreign workers making up 19 percent of the high-skill STEM workforce and 35 percent at the PhD level.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Quantifies dependence: any sustained inflow cut removes irreplaceable talent concentrated at the high-value PhD tier driving R&D.

insights.claim: The US has almost no channel for recruiting skilled workers directly from abroad, so employment-based green cards and H-1B visas mostly retain people already in the country rather than recruit new ones, making graduate school the de facto talent recruitment program.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Because universities are the only effective recruitment pipeline, restricting students closes the main entry point with no substitute mechanism.
insights.quote: the United States has a talent recruitment program; it's called graduate school.

insights.claim: A sustained one-third decline in international student inflows would shrink US real GDP by $240 billion to $481 billion per year over a decade, comparable to erasing the entire economy of Wisconsin or South Carolina.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Frames the macro tail risk to US potential growth; productivity-sensitive sectors and long-duration equity valuations most exposed.
insights.locator: The long-term cost to the US economy

insights.claim: A one-third lower inflow in prior years would leave today's high-skill STEM workforce 6.2 percent smaller overall and 11.5 percent smaller at the PhD level.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Translates visa cuts into concrete workforce shrinkage feeding the productivity model.

insights.claim: Each 1 percentage point rise in high-skill foreign STEM workers' share of the labor force raises annual TFP growth by 0.27 to 0.54 points, so the projected workforce cut lowers annual productivity growth by 0.08 to 0.16 points and leaves GDP 0.79 to 1.57 percent smaller after ten years.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Shows the transmission mechanism from immigration policy to compounding productivity loss; the multiplier is the key sensitivity in the estimate.

insights.claim: The GDP-loss estimate is conservative because it omits tens of billions in direct annual losses to universities and college towns, assumes the decline does not deepen, and assumes no crowding-out reduction in willingness of top talent to come.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Actual damage skews higher than the headline range, raising the macro downside.

insights.claim: Evidence shows neither workers petitioning from abroad nor US-born students would fill the gap, since the 2008 expansion did not crowd out abroad petitioners and native crowd-out occurs only at a handful of fixed-enrollment elite schools.
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Rebuts the main counterargument that domestic or other foreign workers would replace excluded students, supporting the no-substitution assumption.
insights.locator: To be sure, the calculation also assumes

insights.claim: Congress can reverse the damage by guaranteeing visa status for a program's full length, codifying post-graduation work authorization (Keep Innovators in America Act), ranking H-1B by compensation rather than seniority, and clearing the STEM green card backlog (FORTRESS Act).
insights.speaker: Michael A. Clemens
insights.so_what: Watch these specific bills as catalysts that could relieve the talent pipeline risk; passage would be a positive signal for innovation-driven sectors.
insights.locator: US lawmakers can fix this now
